text: The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position with the head elevated at a 25-degree angle. The surgeon stands between the patient's legs, while the camera operator is on the right and the second assistant on the left. Four trocars are inserted into the abdomen, with the laparoscope introduced through a midline port above the umbilicus. The surgeon begins mobilizing the gastric fundus by dissecting the gastroesplenic ligament and dividing the cephalic short gastric vessels. A small fat pad is visible at the end of the gastroesplenic ligament, and the posterior attachments of the upper gastric fundus are divided to open the rear cavity.
The surgeon identifies the posterior window and dissects a space just superior and medial to the free edge of the right crus to facilitate closure of the hiatus.
The surgeon retracts the esophagus anteriorly and to the left while approximating the crura at the level of the aortic dissection.

The surgeon uses forceps to pass behind the esophagus from right to left, grasping the posterior wall of the gastric fundus and pulling it behind the esophagus to form the wrap. A 45 French bougie is inserted to calibrate the fundoplication, followed by placing stitches to secure the wrap, which measures approximately five centimeters in total length.
